one_line_takeaway: A new benchmark (ContrAR, 312 human-validated Meta Quest 3 videos) shows that even the best VLMs detect semantically contradictory AR virtual content only imperfectly (GPT-5 88% but ~19 s latency), with lightweight and text-only detectors near chance.
key_finding: Current VLMs show partial but limited ability to detect AR semantic contradictions, with a sharp accuracy-vs-latency trade-off. GPT-5 was most accurate at 88.14% overall (single-frame) but incurred 19.29 s latency, impractical for interactive AR. Gemini-2.5-Pro reached 83.97% (single-frame) and GPT-4o hit 84.62% (multi-frame) at only ~7.26 s, which the authors flag as the best accuracy/latency balance. Lightweight and open-source models performed near chance on this balanced 50/50 dataset: Qwen-2.5-VL-7B 58.01% (single-frame, 4.14 s), Claude-Haiku-4.5 53.85% (single-frame). The OCR-based text-only GPT-4o baseline scored only 56.41%, showing many contradictions require visual (not just textual) reasoning. Multi-frame prompting helped some models (GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Grok-2-Vision, both Claude, both Qwen: up to +5%) but hurt others (GPT-5, both Gemini, Grok-4: up to −8%) and always increased latency. Human label agreement averaged 4.66/5.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) a formal threat model for "contradictory virtual content attacks" in augmented reality, where a malicious application-level actor renders virtual elements whose semantics conflict with each other (e.g., an arrow pointing left while text says "Turn Right") to mislead or confuse the user; (2) ContrAR, described as the first benchmark dataset for this attack, consisting of 312 real-world AR videos collected on a Meta Quest 3 headset across five application scenarios, with labels validated by 10 human participants; and (3) a systematic evaluation of 11 vision-language models (VLMs) plus an OCR-based text-only baseline on the task of detecting whether a scene contains contradictory virtual content. Note: prior VLM-for-AR-safety systems (ViDDAR, visual-information-manipulation detection, etc.) are cited as Related Work by the same group and others — this paper's distinct claim is the standardized benchmark and threat-model formalization, not those detection systems.
threat_model: Gray-box, application-level semantic attack on AR head-mounted displays. Hardware, OS/compositor, sandboxing, and cloud services are trusted; the user is benign. A malicious AR application has the same privileges as a legitimate one: it can render arbitrary virtual objects/text/overlays within its own app and observe the composited scene (passthrough frames, depth, its own content) via standard APIs, but cannot tamper with sensors, the compositor's blending, other apps' content, inter-app communication, or escalate OS privileges (hardware spoofing and DoS are out of scope). The attacker's goal is to introduce virtual elements with mutually inconsistent semantics to mislead the user, induce confusion, or degrade safety/trust. The defender (attack detection system) is likewise a user-level, view-only process that cannot attribute content to its source app; it must judge, from the composited view alone using VLM-based multimodal reasoning, whether a contradiction exists, ideally in real time. An attack succeeds if contradictory content enters the user's view and remains undetected within a reasonable temporal window.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) dataset scale is modest (312 videos) and may not capture the full diversity of AR environments or device rendering configs; future work plans real-time/procedurally generated adversarial content via 3D generative models and automatic placement rather than manual pre-designed content. (2) User validation captures only offline judgments; the actual behavioral/cognitive impact on users in immersive AR has not been tested — future in-situ HMD studies are planned. (3) Scope is limited to semantic contradiction attacks only, excluding other AR threats like cognitive-overload and distraction attacks. Additional reviewer-observed constraints: the attack content is manually authored by three AR researchers (potential design bias); detection is framed as a binary Yes/No task on sampled frames rather than continuous video (they justify skipping video models due to API/compute limits); the OCR baseline uses only GPT-4o, and "latency" mixes commercial API and HuggingFace-inference-API network conditions, making cross-model latency comparisons imperfect. Note the paper's own prose contains a minor inconsistency, attributing "84.62% multiple frames" to GPT-4.1 whereas Table 2 shows 86.54% for GPT-4.1 multi-frame and 84.62% for GPT-4o multi-frame.

datasets_used: ContrAR (authors' own; 312 real-world AR videos on Meta Quest 3; 156 contradictory / 156 non-contradictory; five apps IN/ON/SI/SA/SR with 54/60/42/90/66 videos; 90 text-only vs 222 visual+textual; 1920x1080, 5-15 s, 30 FPS; validated by 10 human participants, mean agreement 4.66/5)
quantitative_results: GPT-5 best overall: 88.14% single-frame accuracy, latency 19.29 s
GPT-4o multi-frame: 84.62% at 7.26 s (authors' best accuracy/latency balance); single-frame 79.17% at 5.92 s
OCR text-only GPT-4o baseline: 56.41% single-frame, 54.81% multi-frame
Multi-frame prompting: up to +5% for some models, up to -8% for others; all results averaged over 3 runs; latency measured at ~500 Mbps
